title: Shortest Path in Binary Maze
description:
## Shortest Path in Binary Maze

Given an n×n binary grid where 0 = open cell and 1 = blocked, find the shortest path (in number of cells visited) from top-left (0,0) to bottom-right (n-1,n-1).

Movement is allowed in all 8 directions. 
Return -1 if no path exists.

### Example
Input: grid=[[0,0,0],[1,1,0],[1,1,0]]
Output: 4

### Constraints
- 1 ≤ n ≤ 100
- grid[i][j] is 0 or 1

editorial.title: Brute Force Approach
editorial.description: The brute force approach uses recursive DFS to explore all possible paths from (0,0) to (n-1,n-1). At each cell, try all 8 directions. Keep a visited array to avoid revisiting cells in the current path. Track the length of the current path and update the global minimum whenever the destination is reached. Backtrack by marking cells as unvisited when returning from recursion. This explores every possible path which can be exponential in the worst case. It is correct for small grids but very slow for large ones.

title: Task Scheduler with Cooldown
description:
## Task Scheduler with Cooldown

Given a list of CPU tasks (each a letter A-Z) and a non-negative integer n representing the cooldown period, find the minimum number of intervals needed to execute all tasks.

Between two executions of the same task there must be at least n intervals. Idle intervals are allowed.

### Example
Input: tasks=["A","A","A","B","B","B"], n=2
Output: 8

### Constraints
- 1 ≤ tasks.length ≤ 10^4
- tasks[i] is an uppercase English letter
- 0 ≤ n ≤ 100

editorial.title: Brute Force Approach
editorial.description: The brute force approach simulates the CPU scheduler step by step. Count the frequency of each task. At each time slot, find the task with the highest remaining frequency that is also not in cooldown. If such a task exists, execute it and record its last execution time. If no task is available (all remaining tasks are in cooldown), insert an idle slot. Repeat until all tasks are done and count the total number of intervals used. This directly simulates the scheduling process and is easy to understand but slower than the mathematical formula.

title: Minimum Days to Collect All Rewards
description:
## Minimum Days to Collect All Rewards

You have n reward items. Each reward i becomes available on day rewards[i]. You can collect any available rewards on any day, but each reward can only be collected once.

Find the minimum number of days needed so that all rewards can be collected.

### Example
Input: rewards=[1,2,3,2,1]
Output: 3

### Constraints
- 1 ≤ rewards.length ≤ 10^5
- 1 ≤ rewards[i] ≤ 10^9

editorial.title: Brute Force Approach
editorial.description: The brute force approach sorts the rewards array. After sorting, iterate through each reward and compute the minimum day you must wait to be able to collect all rewards up to that point. For index i (0-based) in the sorted array, you need at least day i+1 to have had enough days to collect i+1 items. So the required day for index i is max(rewards[i], i+1). Take the maximum of this value across all indices. This gives the minimum number of days needed to collect all rewards. Sorting makes the reasoning straightforward.

title: Longest Subarray With Sum At Most K
description:
## Longest Subarray With Sum At Most K

Given an array nums of non-negative integers and an integer k, return the length of the longest contiguous subarray whose sum is less than or equal to k.

### Example
Input: nums=[3,1,2,1,1], k=5
Output: 4

### Constraints
- 1 ≤ nums.length ≤ 10^5
- 0 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^4
- 0 ≤ k ≤ 10^9

editorial.title: Brute Force Approach
editorial.description: The brute force approach uses two nested loops. The outer loop fixes the starting index i of the subarray. The inner loop extends the end index j from i onwards, adding each element to a running sum. As soon as the running sum exceeds k, break out of the inner loop since extending further will only increase the sum (all values are non-negative). Track the maximum length (j - i) seen so far whenever the sum is within k. Return the maximum length found. This is O(n^2) overall.

title: Running Average Tracker
description:
## Running Average Tracker

Given an array nums, return an array where the i-th element is the average of all elements from index 0 to i inclusive, rounded to 2 decimal places.

### Example
Input: nums=[1,2,3,4,5]
Output: [1.00,1.50,2.00,2.50,3.00]

### Constraints
- 1 ≤ nums.length ≤ 10^5
- -10^4 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^4

editorial.title: Brute Force Approach
editorial.description: The brute force approach uses two nested loops. For each index i, use an inner loop to sum all elements from 0 to i. Divide by (i+1) to get the average and round to 2 decimal places. Store the result in the output array. This clearly demonstrates what a running average means but is O(n^2) because the sum is recomputed from scratch for each index. The optimal approach maintains a running sum variable to avoid the nested loop entirely.
